quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Alpha decay"?

options.text: ᴬ_Z X → ᴬ⁻⁴_(Z-2) Y + ⁴₂He; massive, +2 charge; stopped by paper; most ionizing; common in heavy nuclei (Z > 82)
1/C_total = 1/C₁ + 1/C₂ + ...; total voltage = sum of individual voltages; same charge on each capacitor
A quantum system exists in multiple states simultaneously until measured; the wave function collapses to one state upon observation
Objects underwater appear shallower than they are due to refraction: apparent depth = actual depth × (n₂/n₁)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Alpha decay" is defined as: ᴬ_Z X → ᴬ⁻⁴_(Z-2) Y + ⁴₂He; massive, +2 charge; stopped by paper; most ionizing; common in heavy nuclei (Z > 82). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Beta-minus decay"?

options.text: F_b = ρ_fluid · V_displaced · g; an object floats when its weight equals the buoyant force (average density < fluid density)
ᴬ_Z X → ᴬ_(Z+1) Y + e⁻ + ν̄; neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino; stopped by aluminum; neutron-rich nuclei
Diagnostic imaging (gamma cameras, PET) and therapy (targeted radiation, brachytherapy) using radioactive isotopes
Node at closed end, antinode at open: f_n = nv/(4L), n = 1,3,5,... (odd harmonics only); e.g., clarinet, stopped pipe

quiz_questions.explanation: "Beta-minus decay" is defined as: ᴬ_Z X → ᴬ_(Z+1) Y + e⁻ + ν̄; neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino; stopped by aluminum; neutron-rich nuclei. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Beta-plus decay"?

options.text: Light behaves as waves (interference, diffraction) and particles (photoelectric effect, Compton scattering); complementary descriptions
Produced by hot dense matter (solids, liquids, dense gases); all wavelengths present; intensity varies with temperature (blackbody)
ᴬ_Z X → ᴬ_(Z-1) Y + e⁺ + ν; proton → neutron + positron + neutrino; occurs in proton-rich nuclei
Zero electrical resistance below a critical temperature T_c; enables powerful electromagnets (MRI: 1.5-7T fields) and lossless power transmission

quiz_questions.explanation: "Beta-plus decay" is defined as: ᴬ_Z X → ᴬ_(Z-1) Y + e⁺ + ν; proton → neutron + positron + neutrino; occurs in proton-rich nuclei. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Gamma decay"?

options.text: A quantum of electromagnetic radiation; energy E = hf; momentum p = h/λ = hf/c; no rest mass
Objects underwater appear shallower than they are due to refraction: apparent depth = actual depth × (n₂/n₁)
Diagnostic imaging (gamma cameras, PET) and therapy (targeted radiation, brachytherapy) using radioactive isotopes
Excited nucleus emits high-energy photon; no change in A or Z; often follows alpha or beta decay; stopped by lead/concrete

quiz_questions.explanation: "Gamma decay" is defined as: Excited nucleus emits high-energy photon; no change in A or Z; often follows alpha or beta decay; stopped by lead/concrete. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Half-life"?

options.text: Restricting wave oscillations to one plane; only transverse waves can be polarized (proves light is transverse)
t₁/₂ = ln(2)/λ; the time for half the radioactive atoms to decay; ranges from nanoseconds to billions of years
When a driving frequency matches a natural frequency of the tube, standing waves form and the sound is amplified dramatically
Peaks at iron-56; lighter nuclei release energy by fusion; heavier nuclei release energy by fission

quiz_questions.explanation: "Half-life" is defined as: t₁/₂ = ln(2)/λ; the time for half the radioactive atoms to decay; ranges from nanoseconds to billions of years. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "ᴬ_Z X → ᴬ⁻⁴_(Z-2) Y + ⁴₂He; massive, +2 charge; stopped by paper; most ionizing; common in heavy nuclei (Z > 82)"?

options.text: Curie temperature
Alpha decay
Huygens' principle
Wien's displacement law

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Alpha decay".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "ᴬ_Z X → ᴬ_(Z+1) Y + e⁻ + ν̄; neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino; stopped by aluminum; neutron-rich nuclei"?

options.text: Beta-minus decay
Gas law special cases
Electric field
Nuclear medicine

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Beta-minus decay".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "ᴬ_Z X → ᴬ_(Z-1) Y + e⁺ + ν; proton → neutron + positron + neutrino; occurs in proton-rich nuclei"?

options.text: Beta-plus decay
Energy levels
Thin-film interference
Apparent depth

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Beta-plus decay".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Excited nucleus emits high-energy photon; no change in A or Z; often follows alpha or beta decay; stopped by lead/concrete"?

options.text: Gamma decay
Thomson's experiment
Rutherford model
Reversible vs. irreversible

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Gamma decay".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "t₁/₂ = ln(2)/λ; the time for half the radioactive atoms to decay; ranges from nanoseconds to billions of years"?

options.text: Wave energy
Rutherford model
Half-life
Sound intensity level

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Half-life".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Radioactivity and Decay is accurate?

options.text: The intensity and spectrum of thermal radiation depend on temperature (Wien's law: λ_max = b/T; Stefan-Boltzmann: P = σAT⁴).
Radioactivity and Decay describes the spontaneous transformation of unstable nuclei.
Magnetic Fields and Forces explores the interaction between magnetic fields and charged particles.
Lenses use refraction to form images.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Radioactivity and Decay as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Radioactivity and Decay?

options.text: Alpha decay (α): nucleus emits ⁴₂He, losing 2 protons and 2 neutrons (A→A-4, Z→Z-2).
Convex mirrors (diverging) always produce virtual, upright, reduced images.
Sound intensity follows an inverse-square law: I ∝ 1/r².
Nuclear stability depends on the neutron-to-proton ratio: light stable nuclei have N ≈ Z; heavier nuclei need more neutrons (N > Z).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Radioactivity and Decay as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Radioactivity and Decay?

options.text: Converging (convex) lenses are thicker in the middle and bring parallel rays to a focus; diverging (concave) lenses are thinner in the middle and spread rays apart.
The kinetic theory of gases derives macroscopic properties from molecular motion.
Beta-minus decay (β⁻): neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino (Z→Z+1).
Both release energy because the products have higher binding energy per nucleon.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Radioactivity and Decay as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Radioactivity and Decay?

options.text: Total internal reflection occurs when light hits the boundary at an angle greater than the critical angle: θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁), and only when going from high-n to low-n medium.
Applications of Quantum Ideas extends quantum principles to technologies and fundamental physics.
Energy carried by waves is proportional to amplitude squared: E ∝ A².
Beta-plus decay (β⁺): proton → neutron + positron + neutrino (Z→Z-1).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Radioactivity and Decay as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Radioactivity and Decay?

options.text: Thin-film interference involves path length differences in reflected light: constructive or destructive depending on the film thickness, wavelength, and indices of refraction.
Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave produced by vibrating objects.
Gamma decay (γ): excited nucleus emits a high-energy photon without changing A or Z.
Mass and energy are interconvertible: the mass defect in nuclear reactions is converted to kinetic energy of products and radiation.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Radioactivity and Decay as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Radioactivity and Decay?

options.text: Musical instruments: standing waves on strings and in air columns produce specific frequencies (harmonics); the length, tension, and density determine the pitch
PET scans use beta-plus emitters (e.g., ¹⁸F): the positron annihilates with an electron, producing two 511 keV gamma rays detected simultaneously to locate tumors
Neon signs: specific gases glow with characteristic colors because their unique energy level spacings produce photons at specific visible wavelengths
Weather balloons expand as they rise because atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude, and Boyle's law predicts volume increase at lower pressure

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Radioactivity and Decay.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Radioactivity and Decay?

options.text: The James Webb Space Telescope uses a 6.5m segmented gold-coated mirror to collect infrared light from the earliest galaxies in the universe
Radon testing: radon-222 (α emitter, t₁/₂ = 3.8 days) seeps from soil into basements; second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking
Atomic clocks use the precise frequency of electron transitions in cesium atoms (9.19 GHz) to define the second—accurate to 1 part in 10¹⁴
Arrow of time: entropy increase explains why ice melts in warm rooms but warm rooms don't spontaneously freeze—the second law governs the direction of all natural processes

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Radioactivity and Decay.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Radioactivity and Decay applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Radiometric dating: uranium-238 (t₁/₂ = 4.5 billion years) decays to lead-206; the U/Pb ratio in rocks determines geological age of the Earth (~4.5 billion years)
Van de Graaff generators accumulate charge on a conducting sphere, creating potentials exceeding 100,000 V—used in nuclear physics and dramatic classroom demonstrations
Noise barriers on highways: effective at blocking high-frequency (short λ) sound but less effective for low-frequency (long λ) sound that diffracts over the top
Wireless charging (Qi standard): a changing magnetic field in the charging pad induces an EMF in the receiving coil inside the phone

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Radioactivity and Decay.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Radioactivity and Decay?

options.text: PET scans use beta-plus emitters (e.g., ¹⁸F): the positron annihilates with an electron, producing two 511 keV gamma rays detected simultaneously to locate tumors
CRT televisions and oscilloscopes: electron beams are steered by electric and magnetic fields to paint images on a phosphor screen
The Sun converts 600 million tons of hydrogen into 596 million tons of helium each second; the 4 million ton mass deficit becomes 3.8 × 10²⁶ W of radiant power via E = mc²
Doppler radar: police speed guns and weather radar measure the frequency shift of reflected radio waves to determine object velocity

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Radioactivity and Decay.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Radioactivity and Decay in practice?

options.text: Camera lens design: modern camera lenses contain 10-20 elements to correct for aberrations, provide zoom capability, and maintain image sharpness across the frame
PET-CT combines metabolic imaging (PET using ¹⁸F-FDG) with anatomical imaging (CT X-rays) for precise cancer diagnosis and treatment monitoring
Radon testing: radon-222 (α emitter, t₁/₂ = 3.8 days) seeps from soil into basements; second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking
Quantum computing uses superposition and entanglement to process information exponentially faster for certain problems (factoring, simulation, optimization)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Radioactivity and Decay.
